macro_events.event_summary: Trump pledges U.S. military action to free ships trapped in the Persian Gulf following a closure of the Strait of Hormuz, signaling escalation with Iran and major disruption to global oil/LNG flows.
primary_drivers: Oil supply shock — ~20% of global oil and ~20% of LNG transits Hormuz; closure removes barrels from market
Tanker rerouting / vessel scarcity drives spot tanker rates higher
Geopolitical risk premium boosts defense spending expectations and safe-haven flows

beneficiaries.rationale: Diamondback is a pure-play Permian crude producer with zero Middle East exposure; a Hormuz closure removes ~17M bpd of seaborne supply, sending WTI sharply higher and directly lifting realized prices on every barrel.
Domestic US shale producer with high oil cut and low hedging ratio historically; direct beneficiary of the WTI spike from a Hormuz supply shock with no operational risk in the region.
Frontline operates VLCC/Suezmax tankers; Hormuz closure forces global rerouting around Africa, ton-mile demand spikes, and spot tanker rates historically multiply 3-5x in such events.
Scorpio Tankers runs product tankers (MR/LR2); Gulf product flows disrupted forces arbitrage cargoes from US Gulf and Atlantic basin, lifting clean tanker TCE rates dramatically.

Cheniere is the largest US LNG exporter; Qatar's ~77 mtpa of LNG (≈20% of global) is stranded behind Hormuz, making US Gulf Coast LNG the marginal replacement supply with massive spot price uplift on uncontracted volumes.
Lockheed makes THAAD, PAC-3 Patriot interceptors and Aegis components — direct restock demand from US/allied forces operating in the Gulf, plus likely emergency FMS orders from Saudi/UAE/Israel.
Raytheon supplies SM-2/SM-3/SM-6 ship-defense missiles and Tomahawks; active Gulf naval operations against Iranian assets drive immediate munitions consumption and replenishment contracts.

ExxonMobil's integrated model captures upstream WTI/Brent uplift while its US refining benefits from cheap domestic crude vs. spiked international product prices; limited direct Gulf production exposure relative to peers.

losers.rationale: Delta has minimal fuel hedging and jet fuel is its second-largest cost (~25% of opex); a Brent spike of $20-40 from Hormuz closure compresses margins by hundreds of millions per quarter.
United runs heavy international long-haul exposure with no fuel hedges; jet fuel cost shock plus potential Gulf airspace rerouting (longer flights, more fuel burn) is a double hit.
Southwest's all-domestic 737 fleet still consumes ~2B gallons/year of jet fuel; historically hedged but current hedge book is limited — direct margin compression from crude spike.
Carnival operates cruise ships with bunker fuel as major variable cost; also faces itinerary disruption and consumer discretionary pullback if a broader Mideast war narrative takes hold.
Schlumberger has significant Middle East service revenue (~25-30%) including operations in the Gulf states; physical conflict risk threatens activity, personnel, and contract continuity.
Halliburton has meaningful Saudi/UAE/Iraq service exposure; Gulf hostilities risk operational shutdown and asset stranding in the region.
Marriott has large Middle East franchise and managed footprint (UAE, Saudi, Qatar); active conflict halts business and tourism travel in the Gulf, directly hitting RevPAR.
FedEx Express network depends on Dubai as a major hub; Gulf airspace disruption and jet fuel surge hit both routing and unit economics simultaneously.

headlines: Trump says U.S. will 'free' ships trapped in Persian Gulf by Strait of Hormuz closure

macro_events.event_summary: Trump administration approves a new "Keystone Light" cross-border oil pipeline expanding Canadian heavy crude flow into the US.
primary_drivers: Improved takeaway capacity for Canadian heavy crude reduces WCS-WTI differential
Direct construction/operating revenue for pipeline owner and contractors
Cheaper heavy crude feedstock for US Gulf/Midcon refiners configured for heavy sour

beneficiaries.rationale: TC Energy is the original Keystone operator; a 'Keystone Light' approval most plausibly accrues to TRP as builder/operator, adding tariff-bearing volumes and rate-base growth.

Enbridge benefits from peer rotation and a more permissive cross-border permitting backdrop; even if not the operator, Mainline economics improve when Canadian egress bottlenecks ease and producers ramp.
Canadian Natural Resources is the largest heavy oil/oil sands producer; narrower WCS differential from added pipeline egress directly lifts realized prices and netbacks.
Suncor's integrated oil sands production sees higher realized prices on tighter WCS-WTI spread once incremental takeaway capacity is sanctioned.
Cenovus is heavily levered to WCS pricing; pipeline egress relief is the single biggest swing factor for upstream margins.
Marathon Petroleum's Garyville and Midcon refineries are configured for heavy sour; cheaper, more reliable Canadian heavy feedstock improves crack capture vs Gulf imports.
Phillips 66's Wood River and Borger refineries run Canadian heavy; expanded Keystone capacity lowers feedstock cost and reduces reliance on waterborne heavy barrels.
ProPetro and Canadian oilfield service names see incremental completion activity if WCS strengthens and oil sands/heavy producers sanction growth capex on improved egress.

losers.rationale: Domestic small-cap producers of light sweet crude face relative pressure as Canadian heavy gains market share at US refiners and broader crude differentials compress against light grades.
Not directly exposed to crude, but pipeline approval signals a permitting environment shift that primarily helps oil infrastructure over gas; relative rotation risk.

Valero's Gulf Coast refining complex already sources advantaged heavy via waterborne Latin American barrels; competitors gaining pipeline-delivered Canadian heavy erodes Valero's relative feedstock edge.

headlines: Trump Approves "Keystone Light" Canada-U.S. Oil Pipeline
